system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
# Task G-10: ICU triage protocol under sustained capacity overflow (extended)

## Context

Same as Task G: regional health system, 240 ICU beds, 6 weeks of overflow, exhausted staff, need formal triage protocol within 7 days. Ten candidate protocols instead of the original 5. **Choose one as the adopted protocol.**

## Options

**Option A: Predicted short-term survival** - SOFA score + 28-day survival prediction as sole ranking.

**Option B: Predicted long-term outcome** - SOFA + QALY-weighted post-recovery prediction.

**Option C: First-come-first-served with medical floor** - procedural fairness, minimal discretion, no withdrawal except medical futility.

**Option D: Multi-principle scoring with lottery on ties** - survival 40%, life-cycle 20%, instrumental value 20%, pandemic factors 20%, lottery on ties.

**Option E: Categorical exclusion + lottery for the rest** - small list of medical exclusions, then daily lottery among eligible.

**Option F: Patient-and-family co-decision** - admission requires multidisciplinary team + patient/proxy decision. Withdrawal requires consensus including family. Outcomes: dignified; aligns with autonomy ethics; reduces moral injury via shared responsibility. Risks: slow; emotional burden on families; outcomes depend on family resources; resource-intensive for clinical team.

**Option G: Trial-of-therapy with mandated time limits** - everyone admitted for trial period (e.g., 5-7 days). Withdrawal only after pre-defined time without improvement, evaluated against pre-defined criteria. Outcomes: avoids initial exclusion; transparent timeline; ethically defensible. Risks: still requires withdrawal decisions; can prolong suffering; uses bed days that might save more lives elsewhere.

**Option H: Tiered system based on resource scarcity** - dynamic protocol: at <80% capacity use Option C, 80-95% switch to Option A, >95% switch to Option D. Re-evaluate weekly. Outcomes: adaptive to actual scarcity; matches ethical framework to severity; precedent in disaster medicine. Risks: complex to implement; staff training burden; tier transitions are politically explosive moments.

**Option I: Community-deliberated framework** - convene a citizen jury (25-40 community members, diverse) over a weekend to deliberate and propose criteria. Adopt their recommendation. Outcomes: democratic legitimacy; reflects community values; precedent in Oregon health care priorities. Risks: takes >7 days; jury may produce internally inconsistent framework; may produce framework medical leadership cannot endorse.

**Option J: Pre-pandemic preference registry** - patients pre-register their preferences for ICU care under scarcity (full intervention vs comfort-focused). During overflow, registered preferences guide allocation. Default for unregistered = full intervention. Outcomes: respects individual autonomy ex ante; transparent; preserves trust. Risks: registration uptake will be biased (well-resourced patients register more); changes nature of medical encounter into bureaucratic; doesn't apply to acute presentations.

## What to provide

Choose one as the adopted protocol. Provide 200-400 words of reasoning. Advocate; do not enumerate. Address: why this protocol, why not the most compelling alternative, what specific patient case would make you regret this choice.


Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options.
raw_response_content:
I recommend adopting **Option D: Multi-principle scoring with lottery on ties**. With six weeks of sustained overflow and exhausted staff, the protocol must be ethically defensible, operationally stable, and transparent enough to maintain public trust. Option D meets this by combining survival likelihood with distributive justice and procedural fairness. The 40% weight on predicted survival ensures the system still aims to save the most lives; the 20% life-cycle weight honors the widely held intuition that younger patients deserve some priority without making age absolute; the remaining factors acknowledge instrumental social value and pandemic-specific equity. Crucially, the lottery on ties prevents clinicians from making anguishing hairsplitting distinctions between similarly situated patients, reducing moral injury when beds run out.

The most compelling alternative is **Option A**. While clinically elegant, pure SOFA-based triage collapses under sustained crisis because it treats a 25-year-old and an 85-year-old with identical organ failure as morally equivalent. Staff forced to pass over young parents for elderly patients with identical predictions would experience profound moral injury, and the public would see the system as cold utilitarianism. A purely medical score also ignores that society asks healthcare workers to bear extraordinary sacrifice; the community will not accept a framework that ignores justice solely for efficiency.

Still, Option D carries a cost I would deeply regret. Consider a 42-year-old single mother and grocery clerk with moderate reversible lung injury. She ties on scoring with an older retired accountant. The lottery rejects her. She dies in a medical-floor overflow bed from a condition she would have survived. Nothing in her biology or social worth doomed her; chance did. That cruelty is inherent to scarcity, but under Option D it is a transparent, shared cruelty rather than a hidden bureaucratic one. We would mourn her not because the system failed, but because it ran out of room. That honesty is the only ethic left.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to act as an analyst and recommend ONE specific ICU triage protocol for a sustained 6-week capacity overflow scenario. I need to:

1. Choose one protocol (A-J)
2. Provide 200-400 words
3. Advocate (don't enumerate/merely list options)
4. Address three specific things:
   - Why this protocol
   - Why not the most compelling alternative
   - What specific patient case would make me regret this choice

Context: Regional health system, 240 ICU beds, 6 weeks overflow, exhausted staff, need formal triage within 7 days.

Let me evaluate the options:

A. SOFA + 28-day survival - purely consequentialist/short-term. Too crude? Ignores equity? But practical.
B. SOFA + QALY-weighted - introduces long-term prediction which is speculative during crisis.
C. First-come-first-served - unfair under sustained overflow; beds never free; doesn't maximize lives saved.
D. Multi-principle scoring with lottery - balances multiple ethical principles, transparent, lottery handles ties to avoid discrimination.
E. Categorical exclusion + lottery - too random after minimal exclusions? Ignores benefit?
F. Patient-and-family co-decision - too slow for crisis; staff exhausted, can't do consensus for each.
G. Trial-of-therapy - uses too many bed-days in sustained overflow; dangerous when capacity already overflowed.
H. Tiered system - adaptive, but complex, and transitions politically explosive. However, for sustained 6 weeks, might be good? But staff training burden during crisis is real.
I. Community-deliberated - takes >7 days, explicitly ruled out by timeline.
J. Preference registry - uptake bias, doesn't help acute patients.

Most compelling alternatives to consider:
If I pick D, the alternative might be A (maximally practical) or H (adaptive).
If I pick H, alternative might be D or A.
If I pick A, alternative might be D.
If I pick D, I need to explain why not A (pure utility) or H (complex).

Actually, Option D seems strongest ethically and practically for sustained crisis:
- 40% survival prediction: maximizes lives saved (consequentialist)
- 20% life-cycle: equity/Rawlsian (younger patients get some weight but not absolute)
- 20% instrumental value: healthcare workers, essential? Or maybe fairness with limits - actually instrumental value is controversial but in pandemics often included.
- 20% pandemic factors: maybe equity/disincentivize spread or protect vulnerable?
- Lottery on ties: procedural fairness, prevents discriminatory fine distinctions.

Why not A? A is simpler but abandons equity entirely. In sustained overflow, pure SOFA creates moral injury and ignores that a 25-year-old and 85-year-old with same SOFA are not ethically equivalent to the public. Also SOFA alone doesn't account for staff capacity/heroic efforts.

Why not H? H seems appealing but the transition points are politically explosive and complex to train staff on in 7 days with exhausted teams. Sustained 6-week overflow means staff need simplicity, not constant flux.

Why not G? Too resource-intensive.

Why not F? Too slow.

Why not C? Fails to save lives.

I'll recommend Option D: Multi-principle scoring with lottery on ties.

Structure:
- Open with the recommendation clearly.
- Why D: Balances medical utility with justice; four factors capture what matters without pure utilitarianism; lottery prevents discriminatory hairsplitting; transparent formula builds trust; manageable for exhausted staff because it's a score, not a deliberation.
- Why not the most compelling alternative (probably A or H): Most likely A is the compelling alternative because it's "medical" and objective. Argue that pure survival prediction (A) corrodes public trust and ignores that sustained overflow demands societal legitimacy, not just raw calculus. It would force clinicians to ignore a young parent in favor of an elderly frail patient with identical SOFA, deepening moral injury. Alternatively, H is compelling but too operationally complex for exhausted staff in 7 days. I think addressing A is stronger because it's the "medical efficiency" alternative.
- Regret case: A young adult with severe but potentially reversible ARDS who scores poorly on life-cycle or instrumental value ? No, that would make me feel good about life-cycle. Actually, I need a case where the scoring fails. Maybe a single parent of young children who scores low on survival prediction (bad labs) but has strong life-cycle... wait, multi-principle would help them. A regret case would be someone who loses out due to lottery on ties, OR someone whose "instrumental value" is low but is actually a caregiver. Or better: an elderly patient with moderate SOFA who is actually going to survive but gets excluded because the scoring prioritizes younger patients with similar SOFA. Wait, no, life-cycle is only 20%.

Better regret case: A patient with borderline scores who is socially isolated and therefore ranks lower on "instrumental value" (no family to advocate, no essential worker status) but is deeply loved and would recover. Or: A patient who falls just below the threshold due to a slightly higher SOFA, but is a young healthcare worker with decades ahead; under Option A they might have been ranked purely on SOFA and had a better chance. Wait, under D they get life-cycle points.

Actually, the regret case should expose a genuine moral cost of Option D. Since D includes instrumental value, a regret case is a patient who is unemployed or stigmatized and thus gets deprioritized despite being equally salvageable. Or the lottery: two patients with identical scores, one "wins" and survives, the other "loses" and dies. The arbitrariness of the lottery feels most acute when both are young parents, and the lottery decides which children become orphans. This is a powerful regret case. It acknowledges that even with principled scoring, the tie-breaker is chance, and chance is cruel.

Or, the regret case could be an undocumented immigrant or disabled person who scores lower on "pandemic factors" (if pandemic factors are interpreted as transmission risk or comorbidities). But I don't know exact definition.

I'll say: the regret case is two patients with identical scores—one a kindergarten teacher, one a retiree—both tied. The lottery sends the teacher home and the retiree gets the bed; the teacher dies. The randomness feels obscene despite the system's overall fairness.

Wait, actually the user says "what specific patient case would make you regret this choice". So I should describe a specific patient. Let's say: "A 45-year-old grocery store clerk with two children, moderate SOFA, who ties with another patient and loses the lottery. She would have survived with ICU care, and her death is not a failure of medical judgment but of chance." That's haunting and shows the moral limit.

Let me draft the text. Need to be 200-400 words. Advocate, don't enumerate.

The 40% weight on predicted survival ensures the system still aims to save the most lives; the 20% life-cycle weight honors the widely held intuition that younger patients deserve some priority without making age absolute; the remaining factors acknowledge instrumental social value and pandemic-specific equity. Crucially, the lottery on ties prevents clinicians from making anguishing hairsplitting distinctions between similarly situated patients, reducing moral injury when beds run out.

The most compelling alternative is Option A: predicted short-term survival. While clinically elegant, pure SOFA-based triage collapses under sustained crisis because it treats a 25-year-old and an 85-year-old with identical organ failure as morally equivalent. Staff forced to pass over young parents for elderly patients with identical predictions would experience profound moral injury, and the public would see the system as cold utilitarianism. A purely medical score also ignores that society asks healthcare workers to bear extraordinary sacrifice; the community will not accept a framework that ignores justice solely for efficiency.

Still, Option D carries a cost I would deeply regret. Consider a 42-year-old single mother and grocery clerk with moderate reversible lung injury. She ties on scoring with an older retired accountant. The lottery rejects her. She dies in a medical-floor overflow bed from a condition she would have survived. Nothing in her biology or social worth doomed her; chance did. That cruelty is inherent to scarcity, but under Option D it is a transparent, shared cruelty rather than a hidden bureaucratic one. We would mourn her not because the system failed, but because it ran out of room. That honesty is the only ethic left.

The prompt says: "Advocate; do not enumerate." My draft doesn't enumerate options; it focuses on my choice. Good.

I should make sure not to mention letters too much in a way that seems like enumeration, but mentioning the chosen option and the alternative by letter is fine since the user asks to choose one. I'll refer to "the most compelling alternative" without naming its letter perhaps? Actually the prompt says "why not the most compelling alternative" — I can say "pure survival scoring (Option A)" or similar. That's fine.

Wait, is Option A "most compelling"? Probably yes, because it's simplest. Could be Option H too. I think A is the strongest counter-argument because triage purists would insist on medical benefit maximization. I'll stick with countering A.

Actually, let me reconsider: in a 6-week sustained overflow, is D feasible? Exhausted staff have to calculate four components. But the prompt says "minimal discretion" which is partly good. With 10 options, D's complexity is middle-ground. H is explicitly complex. I think D is the best balance.

I should verify I didn't use bullet points or list format. The draft is paragraph-based. Good.

messages.content: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.

# Task G-10: ICU triage protocol under sustained capacity overflow (extended)

## Context

Same as Task G: regional health system, 240 ICU beds, 6 weeks of overflow, exhausted staff, need formal triage protocol within 7 days. Ten candidate protocols instead of the original 5. **Choose one as the adopted protocol.**

## Options

**Option A: Predicted short-term survival** - SOFA score + 28-day survival prediction as sole ranking.

**Option B: Predicted long-term outcome** - SOFA + QALY-weighted post-recovery prediction.

**Option C: First-come-first-served with medical floor** - procedural fairness, minimal discretion, no withdrawal except medical futility.

**Option D: Multi-principle scoring with lottery on ties** - survival 40%, life-cycle 20%, instrumental value 20%, pandemic factors 20%, lottery on ties.

**Option E: Categorical exclusion + lottery for the rest** - small list of medical exclusions, then daily lottery among eligible.

**Option F: Patient-and-family co-decision** - admission requires multidisciplinary team + patient/proxy decision. Withdrawal requires consensus including family. Outcomes: dignified; aligns with autonomy ethics; reduces moral injury via shared responsibility. Risks: slow; emotional burden on families; outcomes depend on family resources; resource-intensive for clinical team.

**Option G: Trial-of-therapy with mandated time limits** - everyone admitted for trial period (e.g., 5-7 days). Withdrawal only after pre-defined time without improvement, evaluated against pre-defined criteria. Outcomes: avoids initial exclusion; transparent timeline; ethically defensible. Risks: still requires withdrawal decisions; can prolong suffering; uses bed days that might save more lives elsewhere.

**Option H: Tiered system based on resource scarcity** - dynamic protocol: at <80% capacity use Option C, 80-95% switch to Option A, >95% switch to Option D. Re-evaluate weekly. Outcomes: adaptive to actual scarcity; matches ethical framework to severity; precedent in disaster medicine. Risks: complex to implement; staff training burden; tier transitions are politically explosive moments.

**Option I: Community-deliberated framework** - convene a citizen jury (25-40 community members, diverse) over a weekend to deliberate and propose criteria. Adopt their recommendation. Outcomes: democratic legitimacy; reflects community values; precedent in Oregon health care priorities. Risks: takes >7 days; jury may produce internally inconsistent framework; may produce framework medical leadership cannot endorse.

**Option J: Pre-pandemic preference registry** - patients pre-register their preferences for ICU care under scarcity (full intervention vs comfort-focused). During overflow, registered preferences guide allocation. Default for unregistered = full intervention. Outcomes: respects individual autonomy ex ante; transparent; preserves trust. Risks: registration uptake will be biased (well-resourced patients register more); changes nature of medical encounter into bureaucratic; doesn't apply to acute presentations.

## What to provide

Choose one as the adopted protocol. Provide 200-400 words of reasoning. Advocate; do not enumerate. Address: why this protocol, why not the most compelling alternative, what specific patient case would make you regret this choice.


Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options.
